coredata.dc:title: Novel ZnCo2O4 embedded with S, N-CQDs as efficient visible-light photocatalyst
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Behnood, Reza
Sodeifian, Gholamhossein
coredata.dc:description: The increasing demand for the modern treatment of organic pollutants has led to the development of environment-friendly photocatalytic methods. This paper reports a novel carbon quantum dots-based photocatalyst for the first time. It includes S, N co-doped carbon quantum dots (S, N-CQDs) embedded onto ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles to obtain a potential photocatalyst for the degradation of acid blue 113 (AB 113) under visible light irradiation. The prepared samples were characterized using FE-SEM, EDX, XRD and DRS analyses. The particle size of the prepared samples was seen to be strongly controlled by the amount of capping agent, and the average particle size was found to be 76 nm and 47 nm using 2.0 mL and 4.0 mL PEG, respectively. The DRS and photocatalytic experiments revealed that the S, N-CQDs had a remarkable effect on the band gap and hence the photoactivity of the ZnCo2O4 composite. Minimum band gap was about 2.62 eV for the S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite. After 120 min of visible light illumination, a high level of photodegradation (93 %) was obtained for AB 113 using the combination of S, N-CQDs with ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles. In addition, the prepared S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 photocatalyst showed great stability with only 23 % loss of activity after 6 reaction cycles. The photocatalytic mechanism was studied using different radical scavenging agents. This new photocatalyst provides a promising alternative for organic pollutant degradation applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S, N-CQDs
ZnCo2O4
Photocatalyst
Visible light
Acid blue 113

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of S, N co-doped carbon quantum dots (S, N-CQDs)
Preparation of S, N-CQDs-embedded ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles (S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4)
Photocatalytic study of S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite
Effect of 1 mL S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite dosage
Effect of pH on photocatalytic activity of S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite
Reusability study of S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite
Mechanism of photocatalytic activity of S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of S, N-CQDs (a), ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles (b) and S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite (c).

FE-SEM images of ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles in presence of 2 mL of PEG (a) and 4 mL of PEG (b) and S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite prepared with 2 mL of PEG (c) and 4 mL of PEG (d). TEM image (e) and N2 adsorption- desorption graph (f) for S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4.
EDX spectra of ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles (a), S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite (b) and EDX mapping of the S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite.

DRS spectra for different particle sizes of ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles and S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite (a), and plot of (αhν)2 vs. hν for ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles and S, N-CQDs/ ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite (b).
Photocatalytic degradation of AB 113 over different sizes of ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles.

Photocatalytic degradation of AB 113 over S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite with different amounts of loaded S, N-CQDs. The error bar shows standard deviation of the performed photocatalytic experiments (n = 3).
Photodegradation kinetic curves using bare ZnCo2O4 and S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite.
Photocatalytic activity of AB 113 over different dosages of S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite. The error bar shows standard deviation of the performed photocatalytic experiments (n = 3).

Photocatalytic activity of the S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 photocatalyst for degrading AB 113 with different pH values. The error bar shows standard deviation of the performed photocatalytic experiments (n = 3).
Results of reusability experiments for the S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite toward degradation of AB 113.
EDX spectrum for collected S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite after 6 reaction cycles.
Proposed mechanism for visible-light photocatalytic degradation of AB 113 using the synthesized S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite.
Effect of different radical scavenging agents on photocatalytic degradation of AB 113.
Review ZnO/CDs synthesis and applications except photocatalyst field.

row.entry: ZnO Synthesis Method Nanocomposite Synthesis Method
Used as sensor to Selective Dopamine Detection
Used as sensor for acetic acid gas sensing
Characterizations of sample have been studied.
CO2 photoreduction over the whole UV-Vis-NIR spectrum (400 W Xe Arc lamp)
Used as sensor for different metal ions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Review ZnO/CDs synthesis and applications in photocatalyst field.

row.entry: Photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B under visible light, xenon arc lamp, yield 94% @ 105 min
Photocatalytic degradation of malachite green dye.
light sources: UV, visible and daylight. yield 99 % @ 15 min
Photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine 6G and methylene Blue under UV light and solar light.
Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye and carbendazim (CZ) Fungicide under natural sunlight irradiation. yield 95 % @ 70 min
Photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B under 18 W ultraviolet lamp irradiation, yield 80 % @ 2h.
Photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B under visible light (350 W Xe lamp) and sunlight. Yield 100% @ 15 min.
Photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B under visible light (500 W halogen tungsten lamp). Yield 100 % @ 1h.
Photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B under visible light (AuLight, CELHXF300/ CEL-HXUV300, λ > 400 nm).
Photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B under 18 W ultraviolet lamp irradiation. Yield 90 % @ 2.5h.
Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) under visible light (250 W metal halide lamp (Philips)).
Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) under visible light (300 W Xe lamp). Yield 75 % @ 120 min.
Photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange (MO) under Xenon longarc lamp with 800 W. Yield 90 % @ 120 min.
Photocatalytic degradation of phenol under visible light (500 W Xe arc lamp). Yield 100 % @ 5h.
Photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline under visible light (Xenon lamp). Yield 100% @ 30 min.
Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue under UV light (10 W high pressure Hg lamp) and visible light (white LED lamp). Yield 95 % @ 30 min.
Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue under visible light (500 W Xenon lamp). Yield 90 % @ 4h.
Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue under UV light.
Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue under UV light (10 W high pressure Hg lamp) and visible light (white LED lamp). Yield 90% @ 30 min.
Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue, rhodamine B, and malachite green, under visible, NIR simulated sunlight irradiation (300W xenon lamp with light filter).
Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue in a double-layer glass reactor under daylight simulated irradiation (300W xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300)). Yield 98 % @ 60 min.

head.ce:title: Novel ZnCo2O4 embedded with S, N-CQDs as efficient visible-light photocatalyst

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, University of Kashan, 87317-53153, Kashan, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increasing demand for the modern treatment of organic pollutants has led to the development of environment-friendly photocatalytic methods. This paper reports a novel carbon quantum dots-based photocatalyst for the first time. It includes S, N co-doped carbon quantum dots (S, N-CQDs) embedded onto ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles to obtain a potential photocatalyst for the degradation of acid blue 113 (AB 113) under visible light irradiation. The prepared samples were characterized using FE-SEM, EDX, XRD and DRS analyses. The particle size of the prepared samples was seen to be strongly controlled by the amount of capping agent, and the average particle size was found to be 76 nm and 47 nm using 2.0 mL and 4.0 mL PEG, respectively. The DRS and photocatalytic experiments revealed that the S, N-CQDs had a remarkable effect on the band gap and hence the photoactivity of the ZnCo2O4 composite. Minimum band gap was about 2.62 eV for the S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite. After 120 min of visible light illumination, a high level of photodegradation (93 %) was obtained for AB 113 using the combination of S, N-CQDs with ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles. In addition, the prepared S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 photocatalyst showed great stability with only 23 % loss of activity after 6 reaction cycles. The photocatalytic mechanism was studied using different radical scavenging agents. This new photocatalyst provides a promising alternative for organic pollutant degradation applications.

ce:section.ce:para:
A photocatalytic reaction refers to a photo-induced reaction where a semiconductor is activated by absorbing photons of energy levels equal to or greater than its band gap to produce highly reactive oxygen species such as OH
, O2
ˉ, etc. These highly reactive radicals can then be used in different fields such as degrade organic pollutants or microbial pathogenic contaminations in aqueous media [1,2]. Many semiconductors have been proposed for photocatalytic reaction, including TiO2, ZnO, ZnS, SnO2, CdS, etc. [3–5].

An inherent limitation of common semiconductors is that they need to be activated by light in the UV region. Some other limitations that potentially restrict their large-scale application include fast recombination of charges, low quantum efficiency, low photostability, and high toxicity and production cost [1,6].

In recent years, many strategies have been suggested to overcome these restrictions [6]. In this regard, doping with metallic [7,8] and nonmetallic ions [9,10], coupling of two and more semiconductors [11–14], and preparation of composite materials have opened up new frontiers to researchers who study the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants [5].

Carbon nanomaterials such as CNTs, CDs, grapheme and g-C3N4 are the most promising material for preparation of new composites with superior photocatalytic properties [15–17]. Thanks to their high electron conductivity and large specific surface area, the carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene have been suggested for improving the photocatalytic activity of semiconductors. The recombination of photo-generated charges can be greatly suppressed by excellent electron conductivity of the carbon nanomaterials [18,19]. On the other hand, the large specific surface area of carbon nanomaterials increases the count of activated sites on the surface of the photocatalyst material; the sites can then adsorb organic molecules and, in turn, initiate the photocatalytic reaction [20].

Recently, as a new class of carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received much attention because of their excellent properties such as nontoxicity, high electron conductivity, low cost, low particle size, etc. [21–26]. The combination of CQDs with common semiconductors have promised to produce highly reactive photocatalysts for the mineralization of organic pollutants. For instance, Rahbar et al. prepared S, N co-doped CQDs/TiO2 composite and studied its photocatalytic potential for degrading acid red 88 (AR 88) under visible light irradiation [27]. Sodeifian et al. studied N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO nanocomposite to degrade methylene blue dye under visible light irradiation [28]. Chen et al. succeed to degrade rhodamine B and cefradine under UV irradiation over CQDs/TiO2 photocatalyst [29]. Table 1 presents a summary of the experimental works on the application of ZnO/CDs nanocomposite in other fields than the photocatalysis.

Several researchers investigated ZnO/CDs for photocatalytic application. Qu et al., for example, reported enhanced photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics using N, S doped CQDs-embedded ZnO nanoflowers under sunlight irradiation [42]. Liang et al. sensitized ZnO with CQDs for photodegradation of phenol under visible light irradiation [43]. Some other studies on the photocatalytic applications are listed in Table 2 .

This work reports the first-ever attempt to prepare S, N co-doped CQDs-embedded ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles as a highly reactive photocatalyst for the degradation of acid blue 113 (AB 113) under visible light irradiation. A simple hydrothermal method was used to prepare S, N co-doped CQDs from thiourea and ascorbic acid precursors as sulfur, nitrogen and carbon sources, respectively. Two metallic ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles were prepared using straight and simple co-precipitation reactions in presence of polyethylene glycol (PEG MW: 600) as capping agent. To embed as-prepared S, N co-doped CQDs on the surface of ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles, simple impregnation method was used.

Following with the preparation stage, the S, N co-doped CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite was carefully characterized using FE-SEM, EDX, XRD and DRS analyses. Photocatalytic experiments were performed for the degradation of AB 113 using the prepared photocatalyst under visible light irradiation.

All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. and used as-received without any further purification. These included Zn(NO3)2.6H2O, Co(NO3)2.6H2O, ascorbic acid, thiourea, polyethylene glycol (PEG: 600), and 0.1 M ammonium hydroxide solution.

ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles were prepared using simple co-precipitation method. For this purpose, firstly, appropriate amount of PEG (MW: 600), as capping agent, was dissolved in 40 mL of deionized (D.I.) water (Solution I). Then, 1 mmol of Zn(NO3)2.6H2O and 2 mmol of Co(NO3)2.6H2O were dissolved in D.I. water separately (Solutions II and III). Subsequently, Solutions II and III were added to Solution I in a dropwise fashion and the mixture was stirred for 15 min to obtain a homogeneous solution of zinc and cobalt precursors.

In the next step, ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles were precipitated by adding 0.1 M ammonium hydroxide solution, in a dropwise manner, into the above solution until a pH of 10 was achieved. The solution was then stirred for 1 h to obtain a dark green solid that was collected and washed several times using ethanol and D.I. water and then dried at 100 °C overnight. Next, the dry solid was heated at 500 °C for 120 min to form pure ZnCo2O4 crystals. This synthesis procedure was followed with two different amounts of PEG (2 mL and 4 mL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of S, N co-doped carbon quantum dots (S, N-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for preparing S, N-CQDs started with dissolving 1 mmol of ascorbic acid (carbon source) and 3 mmol of thiourea (nitrogen and sulfur source) in 10 mL of D.I. water followed by stirring the mixture for 10 min to obtain a homogeneous solution. Then, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 6 h to obtain a dark green solution of S, N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of S, N-CQDs-embedded ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles (S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4)
ce:section.ce:para: The S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite was prepared through a simple impregnation reaction between as-prepared S, N-CQDs solution and ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles. For this purpose, appropriate amount of ZnCo2O4 (0.2 g) nanoparticles was dispersed into 10 mL of D.I water followed by introducing different amounts of as-prepared S, N-CQDs solution (0 mL, 0.5 mL, 1.0 mL, 1.5 mL and 2.0 mL) and stirring the mixture magnetically for 5 h. After that, the solid was separated by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 5 min and three-times washed with D.I. water before being dried at 70 °C overnight.

The crystalline structures of the prepared S, N-CQDs powder, ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles and S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite were studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis in the 2θ range of 20°–80° on a Philips X’pert Pro MPD with Cu kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). The surface morphology and elemental composition of the prepared ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles and S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite were studied using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), transmission electron microscope (TEM, Zeiss-EM10C-100 KV), and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis on a Hitachi S4160. BET specific surface area for optimum nanocomposite were measured by using nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms with ASAP micromeritics 2020 at 77 K. Optical properties of the prepared samples were analyzed using UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) on a Shimadzu UV- 670 spectrophotometer.

The photocatalytic activity of the prepared S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite was examined for the degradation of AB 113 under visible light irradiation. For this purpose, photocatalytic experiments were performed by dispersing 50 mg of the photocatalyst into an aqueous solution of AB 113 (50 mL, 20 mg L−1). An OSRAM lamp (HQIBT 400 W/D/DE40FLH1) was used as visible light source.

Here we began with dispersing 50 mg of the photocatalyst into AB 113 solution and magnetically stirring the mix for 30 min to reach an adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst particles and the dye molecules. After that, the photocatalytic reaction was initiated by illumination of dye solution in presence of the photocatalyst. After a certain interval (30 min), the photocatalyst particles were separated from the dye solution by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 5 min. The dye concentration was determined using UV–vis spectrometer at λ max =566 nm.

The crystalline structures of the prepared ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles, S, N co-doped CQDs and S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite were studied by XRD analyses, with the results shown in Fig. 1 . The XRD pattern of S, N co-doped CQDs is shown in Fig. 1a. On this figure, the wide peak centered around 2θ = 26° is clearly attributed to the graphitic carbon (JCPDS file no. 008-0415) [64]. Fig. 1b shows several diffraction peaks at 2θ = 31.49°, 36.96°, 38.68°, 44.98°, 55.65°, 59.41°, 65.27° and 77.35°, which correspond to the diffraction planes for the cubic ZnCo2O4 (JCPDS file no. 001-1149, space group: Fd-3 m) without any impurity. Also, Fig. 1b shows slightly broadening of diffraction peaks with increasing the dosage of the capping agent.

To determine the effect of PEG, as capping agent, on the particle size of ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles, we used the Scherrer’s equation [65] to calculate average crystallite size based on the diffraction peak at 2θ = 36.96°, as follows:
(1)
D =
k λ
β c o s θ
where D is the crystallite size, λ is the X-ray wavelength (Cu kα), β is the half-peak width of the diffraction peak, θ is the diffraction angle at maximum peak and K = 0.89. According to the Scherrer’s equation, the crystallite size of the ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles decreased from 19.3 nm down to 16.1 nm as the added amount of PEG increased from 2.0 mL to 4.0 mL.

Fig. 1c shows the diffraction pattern of the S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite, revealing that the phase structure of the prepared ZnCo2O4 remained unchanged after embedding the S, N-CQDs. Because of high dispersion and low content of the embedded S, N-CQDs on the surface of the ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles, no additional diffraction peaks could be observed for the CQDs [27,66].

The FE-SEM images of the ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles (Fig. 2 a and b) indicate a nano-spherical morphology for the prepared samples. As expected, the particle size of the prepared samples was strongly dependent on the incorporated amount of the capping agent, so that the particle size of ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles decreased with increasing the dosage of PEG. The prepared samples exhibited average particle sizes of 76 nm and 47 nm using 2.0 mL and 4.0 mL of PEG, respectively. The FE-SEM images further indicated some agglomeration of the particles, which is related to thermal treatment of the samples at 500 °C.

The FE-SEM images of the S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite (Fig. 2c and d) proves that the embedded S, N-CQDs on the surface of ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles had no significant effect on the morphology of the ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles, though the embedding seemed to make the ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles more susceptible to agglomeration. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image for the best nanocomposite (1.0 mL S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4) was shown in Fig. 2e to confirm the presence of S,N-CQDs on the surface of ZnCo2O4. As can be seen in the Fig. 2e S, N-CQD with a near spherical-shaped morphology and particle size less than 10 nm adhere to the surface (indicate with white circles).
The BET specific surface area for optimum nanocomposite (1.0 mL S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4) was 61.7 m2 g−1 and the N2 adsorption-desorption graph was shown in Fig. 2f.

The elemental composition and distribution analyses were performed on the ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles and S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite by means of EDX spectroscopy. The EDX spectra of the ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles and S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite (Fig. 3 a and b) showed that the prepared samples were composed, mainly, of zinc, cobalt, oxygen, carbon, nitrogen and sulfur. Demonstrated in Fig. 3c, the elemental mapping of the S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite indicated uniform distribution of carbon, nitrogen and sulfur throughout the composite, clearly confirming the presence of S, N-CQDs on the surface of the ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles.

The optical properties of the prepared samples were studied through DRS analysis. Fig. 4 a shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples. As shown in Fig. 4a, the visible light absorption of ZnCo2O4 was improved upon embedding the S, N-CQDs [67]. Moreover, in both UV and visible regions, the absorption intensity was affected by the particle size; accordingly, an increase in the dosage of the capping agent led to smaller ZnCo2O4 particles and, consequently, significantly lower absorption intensities [1]. The prepared samples had their band gaps (Fig. 4b) increased with the PEG dosage. This was clearly confirmed by the smaller particle size of the ZnCo2O4 that was synthesized using more amount of PEG as capping agent. The band gap of the ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles was further influenced by the introduction of the S, N-CQDs [67]. Indeed, the doping or compositing of a semiconductor (ZnO in this study) by a metal or nonmetal compound (e.g., Co, Fe, Ni, N, S, C or S, N-CQDs, etc.) tend to affect the valence or conduction band of the semiconductor, thereby creating intra-band gap levels and band gap narrowing and hence increasing the absorption of visible light photons. The band gap of pure ZnO is about 3.2 eV. In this study, the ZnCo2O4 and S, N-CQDs/ ZnCo2O4 showed lower levels of band gap energy. In this respect, the calculated band gap of S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite (to visible light region) was effectively lower than that of non-modified ZnCo2O4 [68–70]. As can be seen in Fig. 4b, the minimum band gap was 2.62 eV for S,N-CQDs/ ZnCo2O4 (with 2 mL of PEG).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic study of S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: To study the photocatalytic activity of the prepared samples, we used acid blue 113 (AB 113) as a sample of organic pollutant and visible light irradiation to induce the photocatalytic reaction.

First, we tested the effect of particle size of the prepared ZnCo2O4 on the photocatalytic activity. As mentioned above, the PEG dosage inversely affected the particle size of ZnCo2O4. The sample with a smaller particle size exhibited higher photocatalytic activity, which could be attributed to larger surface area and better dispersion of the photocatalyst particles into the dye solution. Fig. 5 shows the photocatalytic activity of the prepared ZnCo2O4 toward the degradation of AB 113 under visible light irradiation. After 120 min of irradiation, the sample prepared in presence of 4 mL of PEG could degrade some 73 % of the pollutant, while the corresponding figure to the sample prepared by a lower amount of PEG (2 mL) was as low as about 59 %.

To determine the effect of S, N-CQDs on the photocatalytic activity of the prepared ZnCo2O4, we conducted some photocatalytic reactions using S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 samples that were prepared with 4 mL of PEG and different amounts of S, N-CQDs, with the results shown in Fig. 6 . From the results, the optimal degradation performance (about 92.7 %) was achieved with 1.0 mL of S, N-CQDs for sensitization the ZnCo2O4. The photocatalytic activity of the S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite decreased with increasing the embedded amount of S, N-CQDs beyond the optimal value, possibly because of reduced surface area and hence exposure of the ZnCo2O4 photocatalyst to the incident light [71].

The kinetic study of the photocatalytic reaction that leads to the degradation of AB 113 revealed that the reaction could be explained by a pseudo first-order model ( -
ln
C
C 0
= k t , where K is the rate constant of the reaction, t is the irradiation time, and C and C 0 are the AB 113 concentrations after and before the irradiation, respectively. Fig. 7 depicts that the presence of S, N-CQDs on the surface of the ZnCo2O4 imposes a remarkable effect on the photocatalytic activity of the ZnCo2O4 photocatalyst. From the results, the ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles that were sensitized with 1.0 mL of S, N-CQDs were associated with a rate constant of 0.706 min−1, while the bare ZnCo2O4 photocatalyst exhibited a lower constant rate (0.342 min−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of 1 mL S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite dosage
ce:section.ce:para: To study the effect of the dosage of photocatalyst on the degradation of AB 113 under visible light irradiation, we carried out photocatalytic experiments using different amounts (10, 30, 50, 70 and 90 mg) of the nanocomposite photocatalyst for degrading 50 mL of AB 113 solution at 20 mg L−1. Fig. 8 shows the results. According to the figure, optimum performance was achieved with 50 mg of 1.0 mL S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite, beyond which dosage the photocatalytic activity decreased remarkably. The drastic reduction of the photocatalytic activity could be attributed to the reduced light penetration into the dye solution because of increased turbidity of the dye solution at higher dosages of the dispersed photocatalyst [72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on photocatalytic activity of S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the effect of pH of AB 113 solution on the photoactivity of 1 mL S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite, photocatalytic tests were carried with optimum photocatalyst dosage (50 mg) to degrade AB 113 solutions at different pH values.

The results revealed that the prepared photocatalyst tended to exhibit similar photoactivities in the mild acidic and neutral conditions. In this respect, Fig. 9 shows that the AB 113 could be well degraded (by about 93 %) at pH values of 4 and 7, while the photocatalytic efficiency decreased in stronger acidic (pH = 2) and alkaline (pH = 9) conditions.

Because AB 113 is an anionic double azo dye [73], it seems that the negatively charged surface of the photocatalyst is responsible for its decreased photocatalytic activity in alkaline condition. In addition, the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the photocatalyst might be protonated in more acidic conditions. Therefore, the electronegativity and electronic properties of the embedded S, N-CQDs could be clearly affected by the pH value [74,75]. Because of the lower electronegativity of the existing functional groups on the surface of the S, N-CQDs in more acidic condition, the embedded S, N-CQDs onto the surface of ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles could not accept the photo-generated electrons from the conduction bands of the ZnCo2O4 semiconductor, thereby accelerating the charge recombination in acidic condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability study of S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the stability and reusability of the prepared S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite, we conducted 6 cyclic photodegradation reactions under the same set of conditions (50 mg of 1.0 mL S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite and 50 mL of AB 113 at 20 mg L−1). After each reaction, the photocatalyst particles were separated, by centrifuging at 6000 rpm for 10 min, and three-times washed with D.I water and ethanol before being dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of photocatalytic activity of S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para:
Characterization of S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite and its photocatalytic activity toward the degradation of AB 113 under visible light irradiation led us to propose a mechanism (Fig. 12 ) for the photocatalytic reaction based on the experimental results. According to this mechanism, the photo-excitation of ZnCo2O4 particles generates free electrons and holes in the conduction and valence bands of the ZnCo2O4, respectively. The photo-generated electrons can then transfer to highly conductive S, N-CQDs nanoparticles that are closely tied to the surface of the ZnCo2O4 nanoparticles. The transferred electrons can potentially produce highly reactive superoxide radicals through the following reaction [15,27]:
(2)
e -
+
O 2
→
• O
2 -

In addition, the remaining holes in the valence band of the ZnCo2O4 can produce hydroxyl radicals as follows [15,27]:
(3)
h +
+
H 2
O →
O H
•
+
H +
These highly reactive radicals can then mineralize the dye molecules to less dangerous by-products.

A more carful determination of the photocatalytic mechanism was carried out by photocatalytic experiments in presence of different radical scavenging agents. We used the same amounts (2 mM) of different radical scavengers, namely EDTA [76], L-ascorbic acid [77] and tert-butanol [76], for trapping the photo-generated holes, superoxide anions and hydroxyl radicals, respectively. Fig. 13 shows that the photocatalytic activity of the S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite decreased dramatically when using EDTA as radical scavenging agent. This confirms that the photo-generated holes are the dominant radicals for the degradation of AB 113. In other words, the superior photocatalytic activity of the prepared nanocomposite could be clearly attributed to the formation of the photo-generated holes.

In this research, we prepared, for the first time, S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite and evaluated its photocatalytic activity for the degradation of AB 113 under visible light irradiation. UV–vis absorption spectra showed that the S, N-CQDs could increase the light absorption ability of the ZnCo2O4 in both UV and visible light regions. The prepared S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite showed excellent optical and photoactivity properties. Minimum band gap energy (∼2.62 eV) was observed for S, N-CQDs/ZnCo2O4 nanocomposite according to DRS tests. Based on the results of photocatalytic experiments, the samples well met our expectations as some 93 % of the AB 113 could be photodegraded after 120 min of illumination under visible light. In addition, the prepared photocatalyst exhibited good reusability even after 6 reaction cycles. Indeed, the photocatalytic activity of the prepared photocatalyst decreased by only 23 % after 720 min of photocatalytic reaction. Careful study of the reaction mechanism showed that the photo-generated holes were the dominant radicals contributing to improved degradation of AB 113.

Reza Behnood: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Software. Gholamhossein Sodeifian: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Investigation, Supervision, Software, Validation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the research deputy of the University of Kashan-Iran for providing financial support for this project (Grant # Pajoohaneh-1399/15). Moreover, the authors hereby express thanks to the Advanced Materials and Nanotechnology Laboratory of the University of Kashan for providing experimental facilities.

Nayak, A comparative investigation on adsorption performances of mesoporous activated carbon prepared from waste rubber tire and activated carbon for a hazardous azo dye—Acid Blue 113, Journal of Hazardous Materials, 186 (2011) 891-901.
